title: Estrogen Excess: A Case-Based Atlas (20 Published Cases)
subtitle: A Resident Training Module across the seven etiologic buckets — exogenous estrogens/ER agonists, aromatizable androgens & digitalis, peripheral aromatase (obesity/aging/refeeding), familial aromatase excess (AEXS), estrogen-secreting tumours (ACC/Leydig/Sertoli/granulosa), hCG-secreting tumours, and therapeutic hCG — with hormone patterns, the master diagnostic algorithm, and when gynecomastia becomes irreversible.
authors: Resident training module — synthesis of 20 published case reports (see sourceFile); original teaching text
sourceFile: user-provided compilation (Estrogen_Excess_Resident_Training_Module.md), citations verified/corrected via PubMed/PMC search: Correia De Pinho/Aghajanova/Herndon J Reprod Med 2016 PMID 26995893; ESPE 2022 abstract HRP0095P1-382; Sáenz de Rodríguez/Bongiovanni/Conde de Borrego J Pediatr 1985 PMID 3928858; de Carvalho Vilarinho et al. Cureus 2025 PMC12574967; Menon Fertil Steril 2003;79:1659-61; Mahapatra World J Pharm Res 2017; Gerhard/Stratakis JCEM Case Rep 2024 luae050 PMC11040274; Mieritz Indian J Endocrinol Metab 2017 PMC5240059; Braunstein Endotext NBK279105; Giannopoulou et al. Front Endocrinol 2024;15:1487884 PMC11614628; Tan et al. J Pediatr Endocrinol Metab 2019 PMID 30530883; Vrettos et al. Epidemiologia 2025;6(4):64 PMC12551082; Vogt et al. EDM Case Reports 2021 EDM21-0104 PMC8686171; Eze et al. J West Afr Coll Surg 2023 PMC10914104 PMID 38449551; Lefèvre/Bouvattier et al. J Pediatr Endocrinol Metab 2005 PMID 15947469; Ait Benkacem et al. Sch J Med Case Rep 2025;13(9):1991-4; McDonnell/Mc Donald/Behan/Gibney J Endocr Soc 2021 PMC8266101; Eren et al. J Clin Res Pediatr Endocrinol 2010 PMC3005758; Devos et al. Case Rep Oncol Med 2024 8731806 PMC10838210 PMID 38314227; bonus iatrogenic-hCG case is a representative vignette (gonadotropin-therapy literature, e.g. PMID 32445446)

relatedChapters.title: Pubertal Gynecomastia That Is Not Physiological (3 cases)

patient.age: 2.5–72 years (20 published cases + 1 therapeutic-hCG bonus case)
patient.sex: Male-predominant; includes one postmenopausal woman (granulosa-cell tumour) and a Puerto Rico paediatric cluster
patient.chiefComplaint: Estrogen excess along its final common pathway — gynecomastia, peripheral precocious puberty, or postmenopausal bleeding
pmh: Bucket 1 — Exogenous estrogens: indirect transfer of maternal compounded cream (Wiley protocol) and a transgender parent's estradiol gel; estrogen-contaminated food (Puerto Rico cluster)
Bucket 2 — Aromatizable androgens & digitalis: anabolic-steroid abuse (gynecomastia, azoospermia, lasting hypogonadism); spironolactone+digoxin; finasteride
Bucket 3 — Peripheral aromatase: obesity, aging triad, refeeding after malnutrition
Bucket 4 — Familial aromatase excess (AEXS): 15q21 CYP19A1 deletion and a novel duplication
Bucket 5 — Estrogen-secreting tumours: feminizing adrenocortical carcinoma (×2), Leydig-cell tumour, large-cell calcifying Sertoli-cell tumour in Peutz–Jeghers, ovarian granulosa-cell tumour
Bucket 6 — hCG-secreting tumours: metastatic germ-cell tumour with hCG hyperthyroidism, hepatoblastoma, pulmonary choriocarcinoma
Bucket 7 — Therapeutic hCG: gonadotropin therapy for hypogonadotropic hypogonadism
medications: Causative agents across cases: compounded/transdermal estradiol, anabolic-androgenic steroids, spironolactone, digoxin, finasteride, therapeutic hCG/hMG

caseDescription: A case-based atlas of estrogen excess organised by the seven-bucket Williams Textbook framework. Estrogen excess in men, prepubertal children, and (inappropriately) in women funnels into one final common pathway — gynecomastia, gonadotropin-independent precocious puberty, or postmenopausal bleeding — but the upstream causes range from trivially reversible (a parent's topical estrogen, an offending drug, obesity) to life-threatening (feminizing adrenocortical carcinoma, hCG-secreting germ-cell tumours). The unifying diagnostic logic is the hormone pattern: estradiol relative to testosterone, the gonadotropin response (suppressed by autonomous estrogen/androgen sources, high in primary hypogonadism), and the β-hCG/AFP axis, refined by LC-MS/MS steroid profiling and targeted imaging. The module pairs 20 published cases with a master algorithm and a board-style question bank.
keyFindings: One final common pathway (gynecomastia / peripheral precocious puberty / postmenopausal bleeding); seven distinct etiologic buckets behind it
The estradiol-to-testosterone RATIO, not absolute estradiol, drives gynecomastia — and local tissue aromatase can act with a normal serum E2
Gonadotropin pattern localises: suppressed LH/FSH = autonomous estrogen/androgen source; high LH/FSH = primary hypogonadism
β-hCG elevation (with the high-dose hook effect caveat) points to germ-cell, hepatic, or pulmonary trophoblastic tumours
Drug-induced and AAS gynecomastia can become irreversible (fibrosis) after ~12 months — treat early with a SERM
finalDiagnosis: Estrogen excess is a syndrome, not a diagnosis: management is entirely cause-specific. Remove the offending exposure/drug (exogenous estrogen, AAS, spironolactone/digoxin, finasteride) or correct the physiologic driver (weight loss, time for refeeding); excise estrogen-secreting tumours (adrenalectomy for feminizing ACC, testis-sparing or radical surgery for gonadal stromal tumours, TAH-BSO for granulosa-cell tumour); treat trophoblastic tumours with chemotherapy (BEP/PLADO) — letting hCG-driven endocrinopathies resolve with tumour control rather than chasing them; use aromatase inhibitors for AEXS and local-aromatase tumours (LCCSCT) and to preserve height; and reserve SERM/surgery for established gynecomastia. LC-MS/MS steroid profiling and disciplined use of the gonadotropin/β-hCG pattern drive the work-up.
learningObjectives: Classify estrogen excess into the seven etiologic buckets and map a presentation to the likely bucket
Use the estradiol:testosterone ratio, gonadotropin pattern, and β-hCG/AFP to localise the source
Recognise the must-not-miss tumours (feminizing ACC, Leydig/Sertoli, granulosa-cell, germ-cell/hCG) and their imaging triggers
Diagnose AEXS (chromosomal microarray for 15q21) and apply aromatase inhibitor ± GH for height preservation
Time treatment correctly — SERM within ~12 months, surgery for fibrotic disease, tumour control before chasing hCG-driven endocrinopathies

items.title: How estrogen excess presents — one final common pathway, seven etiologic buckets
items.content: Estrogen excess looks the same downstream however it is generated. In men it produces gynecomastia, low libido, erectile dysfunction, testicular atrophy and infertility; in prepubertal boys, gonadotropin-independent (peripheral) precocious puberty or isolated thelarche with advanced bone age and threatened final height; in women, dysfunctional or postmenopausal bleeding, endometrial hyperplasia/carcinoma, or isosexual precocity in girls. The Williams Textbook framework sorts the upstream causes into seven buckets: (1) exogenous estrogens / estrogen-receptor agonists; (2) aromatizable androgens and digitalis; (3) increased peripheral aromatase activity (obesity, aging, refeeding, cirrhosis); (4) familial/genetic aromatase excess (CYP19A1 rearrangements, AEXS); (5) estrogen-secreting tumours (adrenocortical carcinoma, Leydig, Sertoli, granulosa-cell); (6) hCG-secreting tumours (germ-cell, hepatoblastoma, lung, urothelial); and (7) therapeutic hCG. Holding this map in mind turns a vague 'high estrogen' into a directed work-up.
keyPoints: Final common pathway: gynecomastia / peripheral precocious puberty / abnormal uterine bleeding
Seven buckets: exogenous E, aromatizable androgens+digitalis, peripheral aromatase, AEXS, estrogen-secreting tumours, hCG tumours, therapeutic hCG
The bucket map directs which labs and imaging to order

items.title: Why the estradiol-to-testosterone ratio, not absolute estradiol, drives gynecomastia
items.content: Gynecomastia is the breast's response to an estrogen-to-androgen imbalance, so it usually requires a shift in the E2:T ratio rather than a dramatic absolute rise in estradiol. Aromatase (CYP19A1) sets the numerator by converting androstenedione to estrone and testosterone to estradiol; in adult men adipose tissue is the dominant extra-gonadal aromatase site, so anything that increases fat mass, aromatase activity, or androgen substrate tilts the ratio. SHBG modifies the free fractions — when SHBG falls (obesity) free estradiol rises relative to free testosterone, and when it rises (aging, cirrhosis) it binds testosterone preferentially, again raising the free E2:T ratio. Crucially, tissue-level aromatase (in a Sertoli-cell tumour, or 'spared' testosterone after 5α-reductase blockade) can drive breast growth with a serum estradiol that looks normal. This is why interpreting estradiol in isolation misleads, and why the gonadotropin pattern and the clinical context matter more than a single number.
keyPoints: Gynecomastia tracks the E2:T ratio, not absolute E2
Adipose aromatase and SHBG shifts move the ratio; substrate (androgen) excess feeds it
Local tissue aromatase can cause gynecomastia with a normal serum estradiol

items.title: Why exogenous topical estrogen gives a 'breast-up, gonadotropins-down' signature (Cases 1–2)
items.content: Two paediatric cases show indirect transfer. An 8-year-old boy developed Tanner-B2 gynecomastia and ~1.5-year advanced bone age from sleeping beside a mother applying a compounded 'Wiley protocol' estradiol/estrone/progesterone cream (Correia De Pinho, Aghajanova & Herndon, J Reprod Med 2016; PMID 26995893); a 7-year-old developed gynecomastia and peripheral precocious puberty from a transgender parent's estradiol gel (5 actuations = 3.75 mg/day) during daily lap contact (ESPE 2022, abstract HRP0095P1-382). Both showed the exogenous signature: prepubertal/suppressed LH and FSH, low testosterone (<10 ng/dL), and estradiol only mildly elevated for age, with normal DHEAS/hCG/AFP and normal gonadal and adrenal imaging. Exogenous estrogen feeds back to switch off gonadotropins while stimulating breast and bone directly — so the testes stay prepubertal even as the breast grows. Management is to remove the source: change the parent's application site, enforce hand-washing and covered skin for ≥4 hours, or switch a gel to an occlusive patch (gels transfer; patches and oral routes do not). Tissue regressed and estradiol normalised within ~6 months without drugs or surgery.
keyPoints: Signature: suppressed LH/FSH + low T + mildly high-for-age E2 + prepubertal testes
Gels are the high-risk vehicle for skin-to-skin transfer; patches/oral routes do not transfer
Remove the source first — regression in months; AIs/surgery rarely needed

items.title: How a community cluster of premature thelarche points to environmental estrogen (Case 3)
items.content: Between 1979 and 1985 Puerto Rican paediatricians documented hundreds of girls aged 6 months–8 years with isolated thelarche, vaginal bleeding or peripheral precocious puberty, plus prepubertal boys with gynecomastia (Sáenz de Rodríguez, J Pediatr 1985). Work-up was uniform: mildly elevated estradiol-equivalent activity, suppressed LH/FSH (a peripheral pattern), normal hCG and normal imaging — i.e., a non-tumoural exogenous source. Chromatographic analysis of locally produced whole milk, chicken and beef revealed estradiol-equivalent activity from illegal growth-promoting estrogens (DES, zeranol) in livestock; children switched to imported milk or organic poultry regressed within 3–6 months. The lesson is epidemiological: a cluster of premature thelarche should trigger an environmental/food-safety investigation, and every such child needs a dietary history (milk/meat source, soy and herbal supplements). Endocrine-disrupting compounds — DDT, BPA, phthalates, lavender/tea-tree oil — remain a live differential worldwide.
keyPoints: A cluster of premature thelarche = environmental/food investigation, not just individual work-up
Peripheral pattern (suppressed gonadotropins, normal hCG/imaging) with a dietary source
Take a dietary/EDC history; regression follows removal of the source

items.title: Why anabolic-androgenic steroids cause gynecomastia and lasting hypogonadism (Cases 4–5)
items.content: Supraphysiologic exogenous testosterone is a substrate for adipose aromatase, so it generates markedly elevated estradiol that both stimulates breast ducts and (with the androgen itself) suppresses LH and FSH — producing secondary hypogonadism that can persist long after the steroids stop. A 30-year-old bodybuilder on testosterone cypionate 500 mg/week for 5 years had total T 1840 ng/dL with suppressed gonadotropins, E2 78 pg/mL, polycythaemia (HCT 56%), dyslipidaemia and transaminitis (de Carvalho Vilarinho et al., Cureus 2025; PMC12574967); after cessation he developed anabolic-steroid-induced hypogonadism (ASIH). The key teaching trap is infertility: a 31-year-old professional bodybuilder was azoospermic despite serum T 920 ng/dL, because exogenous androgen suppresses intratesticular testosterone and FSH-dependent spermatogenesis (Menon, Fertil Steril 2003). Recovery used hCG 5000 IU twice weekly (driving Leydig cells/intratesticular T) plus hMG 75 IU thrice weekly (FSH for Sertoli cells); sperm reappeared at 4 months and his partner conceived at 9 months. Always check LH/FSH in a young man with gynecomastia and high-normal/high testosterone — suppressed gonadotropins with high T means an exogenous source. SERM (tamoxifen) early, because AAS gynecomastia fibroses and becomes irreversible after ~12 months.
keyPoints: Exogenous T → adipose aromatisation → high E2 + HPG suppression (ASIH persists after stopping)
Azoospermia despite high serum T — intratesticular T and FSH are suppressed; recover with hCG + hMG
High T + suppressed LH/FSH = exogenous source; treat gynecomastia with a SERM before fibrosis sets in

items.title: How spironolactone and digoxin combine into an estrogen-dominant milieu (Case 6)
items.content: A 54-year-old man with dilated cardiomyopathy on digoxin 0.25 mg/day plus spironolactone 50 mg/day developed painful grade-III gynecomastia over 6 weeks, with low-normal testosterone, high estradiol (62 pg/mL) and inappropriately normal gonadotropins (Mahapatra, World J Pharm Res 2017). Two mechanisms synergise: digoxin/digitoxin are structurally estradiol-like and weakly agonise the estrogen receptor, while spironolactone displaces testosterone from SHBG, blocks the androgen receptor, and inhibits 17α-hydroxylase — lowering testosterone and raising estradiol. The fix is rational substitution: replace digoxin (here with amiodarone) and switch spironolactone to eplerenone, which does not bind the androgen receptor and rarely causes gynecomastia; the breast tissue regressed substantially by 4 months. Whenever gynecomastia appears in a CHF or cirrhosis patient, audit the polypharmacy — spironolactone, digoxin, cimetidine/H2-blockers, calcium-channel blockers, PPIs, antiretrovirals and ketoconazole are repeat offenders.
keyPoints: Digoxin = weak ER agonist; spironolactone lowers T (AR block, 17α-hydroxylase inhibition, SHBG displacement)
Switch to eplerenone (no AR binding) and replace digoxin — regression in months
Audit CHF/cirrhosis polypharmacy: spironolactone, digoxin, cimetidine, CCBs, PPIs, antiretrovirals, ketoconazole

items.title: Why brief finasteride exposure can cause irreversible gynecomastia despite a normal serum estradiol (Case 7)
items.content: A 20-year-old man took finasteride 1 mg/day for hair loss for only 2 months, developed a 3-cm subareolar nodule, and still had gynecomastia 5 months after stopping (Gerhard & Stratakis, JCEM Case Reports 2024). His testosterone, LH and FSH were normal, estradiol was high-normal (38 pg/mL) and DHT was low — the signature of 5α-reductase inhibition. The mechanism is local: blocking conversion of testosterone to DHT leaves more testosterone to be aromatised to estradiol, so the breast experiences relative hyperestrogenism even though serum E2 looks unremarkable. Pharmacovigilance shows ~20% of FDA-reported finasteride-gynecomastia cases did not regress after stopping. Treatment timing is everything: for early (<12 months), painful, non-fibrotic gynecomastia a SERM (tamoxifen 10–20 mg/day or raloxifene 60 mg/day) is first-line with 60–80% response; established fibrotic disease (>12 months) needs surgery. This patient achieved only partial regression on raloxifene and required reduction mammoplasty.
keyPoints: 5α-reductase block → low DHT, spared T aromatised → local hyperestrogenism with normal serum E2
~20% of finasteride gynecomastia does NOT regress after stopping — it can be irreversible
SERM first-line if <12 months/non-fibrotic; surgery for established fibrotic tissue

items.title: How obesity amplifies peripheral aromatase (Case 8)
items.content: A 14-year-old boy with BMI 31.4 had 18 months of soft bilateral gynecomastia, low-normal testosterone, elevated estradiol (42 pg/mL), low SHBG, and hyperinsulinaemia (Mieritz, Indian J Endocrinol Metab 2017). Adipose tissue is rich in aromatase, and with obesity both enzyme mass and activity rise — increasing conversion of testosterone to estradiol and androstenedione to estrone; leptin further upregulates aromatase, and low SHBG raises the free estradiol reaching breast tissue. Structured weight loss is first-line: an 11-kg loss over 9 months raised testosterone to 510 ng/dL, dropped estradiol to 24 pg/mL and halved breast diameter, with the residual glandular core excised only after weight stabilised. Two practical points: distinguish true gynecomastia (a firm subareolar disc ≥0.5 cm, felt supine with a thumb-and-finger pinch) from pseudogynecomastia (adipose only), and screen these patients for metabolic syndrome and pre-diabetes.
keyPoints: Obesity ↑ aromatase mass/activity + ↓ SHBG → relative hyperestrogenism
Weight loss first-line; surgery only after weight stabilises
Pinch test supine to separate true gynecomastia from pseudogynecomastia; screen for dysglycaemia

items.title: Why aging shifts the estrogen-androgen balance — the triad (Case 9)
items.content: A 72-year-old man had 3 years of bilateral breast enlargement with low testosterone, low free T, high-normal estradiol, markedly elevated SHBG (76 nmol/L) and high LH/FSH (primary hypogonadism), plus femoral-neck osteopenia (Braunstein, Endotext NBK279105). Three age-related changes converge — the 'triad of aging': falling Leydig-cell testosterone production; rising adipose mass with age-related aromatase upregulation raising estradiol; and rising SHBG that preferentially binds testosterone, so free T falls disproportionately and the free E2:free T ratio climbs. Up to 65% of men over 60 have palpable breast tissue, so screen for symptoms rather than reacting to the exam alone. Two safety nets: always exclude male breast cancer when a mass is unilateral, eccentric, hard or fixed (mammography ± biopsy), and look for co-existing contributors — drugs (CCBs, H2-blockers, PPIs), chronic kidney/liver disease, occult malignancy. This patient declined testosterone (family history of prostate cancer) and had partial regression on low-dose tamoxifen.
keyPoints: Triad: ↓ Leydig T, ↑ adipose aromatase, ↑ SHBG → ↑ free E2:free T
Up to 65% of men >60 have palpable breast tissue — screen on symptoms
Exclude breast cancer if unilateral/hard/eccentric/fixed; review drugs and organ disease

items.title: How refeeding after starvation reproduces pubertal gynecomastia (Case 10)
items.content: A 22-year-old man lost 18 kg from untreated coeliac disease, then regained 14 kg on a gluten-free diet and developed painful gynecomastia and morning erections — a 'second puberty' (Braunstein, Endotext 2023). Starvation causes functional central hypogonadism (low LH/FSH/T); refeeding re-activates the HPG axis the way puberty does, with estradiol rising ahead of testosterone and a transient increase in the E2:T ratio (his post-refeeding E2 overshot to 56 pg/mL before normalising). The same mechanism explains gynecomastia in cirrhotic patients after transplant, in recovering anorexia nervosa, severe IBD, and historically in liberated prisoners-of-war. The management is reassurance: refeeding gynecomastia self-resolves over 1–2 years; surgery is considered only if it persists beyond 18–24 months.
keyPoints: Refeeding re-activates the HPG axis like puberty — E2 rises before T, raising the E2:T ratio transiently
Same pattern post-transplant, recovering anorexia/IBD, historical POWs
Self-limited — reassure; surgery only if persisting >18–24 months

items.title: Why aromatase excess syndrome (AEXS) should be suspected and how to confirm it (Cases 11–12)
items.content: Aromatase excess syndrome is an autosomal-dominant disorder in which genomic rearrangements at 15q21.2 place a cryptic promoter upstream of CYP19A1, causing constitutive aromatase overexpression in skin, fat and lymphocytes. Suspect it with the tetrad: pre-/peri-pubertal-onset gynecomastia, advanced bone age, suppressed gonadotropins with low testosterone and inappropriately normal/high estradiol, and a dominant family history. A 15q21 microdeletion family showed an 8-year-old with B3 gynecomastia and bone age 11; a treated younger brother on letrozole from age 7 reached adult height 179 cm versus a predicted 162 (Giannopoulou et al., Front Endocrinol 2024;15:1487884). A separate Chinese boy had a novel de novo 1.1-Mb 15q21.2 duplication (Tan et al., J Pediatr Endocrinol Metab 2019; PMID 30530883) — proving that a negative family history does not exclude AEXS. Diagnosis needs chromosomal microarray targeting 15q21.2; sequencing alone often misses the rearrangement. Treatment is a third-generation aromatase inhibitor (letrozole or anastrozole) — well tolerated, preserves adult height, and restores virilisation; AI plus growth hormone is an emerging height-preserving combination (with DXA and spine monitoring for AI bone effects in children).
keyPoints: Tetrad: peripubertal gynecomastia + advanced bone age + suppressed LH/FSH with low T & normal/high E2 + AD family history
Diagnose by chromosomal microarray for 15q21.2 (sequencing misses rearrangements); de novo cases exist
Treat with letrozole/anastrozole ± GH to preserve height; monitor DXA/spine in children

items.title: Why feminizing adrenocortical carcinoma must be excluded — and how LC-MS/MS finds it (Cases 13–14)
items.content: Feminizing adrenocortical carcinoma is the rare but classic estrogen-secreting tumour — under 2% of adrenocortical neoplasms, but over 80% malignant in adults. Suspect it in any man with rapid-onset gynecomastia, an abdominal mass, and suppressed gonadotropins. A 24-year-old had infertility, painful gynecomastia, E2 90 pg/mL, low T, suppressed gonadotropins, an 8.2-cm right adrenal mass and inappropriately preserved cortisol despite suppressed ACTH; pathology was ACC (Weiss 6, Ki-67 10%) with aromatase-positive immunohistochemistry confirming autonomous estradiol production (Vrettos, Epidemiologia 2025). A 57-year-old was nearly mislabelled a prolactinoma until LC-MS/MS plasma steroid profiling revealed the ACC signature — elevated estradiol, estrone, androstenedione, 11-deoxycortisol, 17-OH-pregnenolone and DHEA (Vogt et al., EDM Case Reports 2021; PMC8686171). Two lessons: conventional estradiol immunoassays cross-react with adrenal steroid metabolites and mislead, so LC-MS/MS is the gold standard when ACC is suspected and is now used to triage adrenal incidentalomas; and treatment is R0 adrenalectomy, with adjuvant mitotane ± EDP chemotherapy reserved for high-risk pathology (Ki-67 >20%, vascular invasion). Surveillance is mandatory — recurrence exceeds 50% even after complete resection.
keyPoints: Rapid gynecomastia + abdominal mass + suppressed gonadotropins → exclude feminizing ACC (>80% malignant in adults)
LC-MS/MS steroid signature (estrogens + adrenal precursors) beats misleading immunoassays
R0 adrenalectomy is cornerstone; mitotane±EDP for high-risk; lifelong surveillance (recurrence >50%)

items.title: Why both testes must be imaged even when the exam is normal — Leydig-cell tumour (Case 15)
items.content: A 39-year-old with 3 years of gynecomastia, low libido and infertility had markedly elevated estradiol (179.9 pg/mL), low testosterone, suppressed LH/FSH, and normal β-hCG/AFP/LDH (excluding germ-cell tumour); he could not feel a testicular mass himself, but careful bimanual exam and ultrasound found a 2–3-cm lower-pole left-testis lesion (Eze, J West Afr Coll Surg 2023). About 25% of Leydig-cell tumours predominantly secrete estradiol via intratumoural aromatase rather than testosterone. Testis-sparing enucleation normalised estradiol, restored testosterone (480 ng/dL) and improved sperm concentration by 4 months; histology was benign (inhibin/calretinin positive, Ki-67 <3%, no malignant features). The rule: always image both testes in a man with gynecomastia and suppressed gonadotropins, because small Leydig tumours are frequently non-palpable. Testis-sparing surgery suits benign-appearing tumours under ~2.5 cm in fertility-sparing scenarios; otherwise radical orchiectomy is standard. About 10% of adult Leydig-cell tumours are malignant — predictors are size >5 cm, age >50, mitoses >3/10 HPF, vascular invasion and necrosis.
keyPoints: Gynecomastia + suppressed gonadotropins → scrotal ultrasound even if exam is normal (small tumours non-palpable)
~25% of Leydig tumours are estradiol-predominant (intratumoural aromatase)
Testis-sparing surgery for benign <2.5 cm; ~10% malignant — size>5cm, age>50, mitoses, vascular invasion, necrosis

items.title: How large-cell calcifying Sertoli-cell tumour presents in Peutz–Jeghers syndrome (Case 16)
items.content: A 7-year-old boy had a year of painless B3 gynecomastia (7-cm disc), mucocutaneous pigmented macules and a family history of intussusception — Peutz–Jeghers syndrome (STK11) — with bilateral testicular microcalcifications on ultrasound (Lefevre, JPEM 2005). His estradiol was only just above the prepubertal range (12 pg/mL) with suppressed T/LH/FSH and markedly elevated inhibin B. The apparent paradox — big breasts, 'normal' estradiol — is explained by local aromatase overexpression in large-cell calcifying Sertoli-cell tumour (LCCSCT) cells, acting in paracrine fashion on the testis-pituitary axis and peripheral tissues without large rises in circulating E2. Bilateral wedge biopsies confirmed intratubular large-cell hyalinising Sertoli-cell tumours; an aromatase inhibitor (testolactone, then anastrozole 1 mg/day) shrank the breast disc from 7 to 3 cm and halted bone-age advance over 12 months, with orchiectomy reserved for malignant or large lesions. LCCSCT is the classic cause of prepubertal gynecomastia in PJS — virtually pathognomonic with bilateral testicular microcalcifications — and PJS itself demands coordinated GI/pancreatic/breast/lung/gonadal tumour surveillance.
keyPoints: Prepubertal gynecomastia + testicular microcalcifications + mucocutaneous macules = LCCSCT in PJS
Estradiol may be 'normal' — local aromatase acts in paracrine fashion; inhibin B is a useful marker
Aromatase inhibitor first-line; orchiectomy for malignant/large lesions; PJS needs multi-organ surveillance

items.title: Why postmenopausal bleeding with suppressed FSH and high estradiol means a granulosa-cell tumour (Case 17)
items.content: A 56-year-old postmenopausal woman had 4 months of heavy painless bleeding and breast tenderness with atypical endometrial hyperplasia on biopsy, a 22-mm endometrium, estradiol 168 pg/mL (postmenopausal reference <30), elevated inhibin B (480 pg/mL) and AMH (12 ng/mL), and an inappropriately suppressed FSH (3 IU/L — too low for menopause because tumoural estrogen feeds back) (Ait Benkacem, Sch J Med Case Rep 2025). Initial transvaginal scan showed no adnexal mass; MRI found a subtle 3.5-cm left-ovarian lesion, and surgery (TAH-BSO with staging) confirmed an adult granulosa-cell tumour (FIGO IC; coffee-bean nuclei, Call-Exner bodies, inhibin/calretinin positive) with concurrent atypical hyperplasia but no invasive carcinoma. Three teaching points: postmenopausal bleeding + suppressed FSH + high estradiol should prompt a hunt for an estrogen-secreting ovarian tumour even when imaging is initially 'normal'; inhibin B and AMH are the most sensitive granulosa-cell markers and outperform CA-125 for recurrence; and up to 20% of adult GCTs have concurrent endometrial hyperplasia or carcinoma, so endometrial sampling is mandatory. Adult GCTs are indolent but recur late (5–20 years) — counsel lifelong surveillance.
keyPoints: Postmenopausal bleeding + suppressed FSH + high E2 → estrogen-secreting ovarian tumour (image beyond a normal TVS)
Inhibin B and AMH outperform CA-125 for granulosa-cell tumour detection/recurrence
≤20% have concurrent endometrial hyperplasia/carcinoma — sample the endometrium; recurs late, follow for life

items.title: Why multiple simultaneous endocrinopathies in a young man signal a trophoblastic tumour (Case 18)
items.content: A 24-year-old man had painful gynecomastia, thyrotoxicosis (TSH <0.05, free T4 29.5, TPO-negative), suppressed LH/FSH yet very high testosterone (>1500 ng/dL), a huge estradiol (~1070 pg/mL), high prolactin and SHBG, and β-hCG 503,944 IU/L — from a metastatic non-seminomatous germ-cell tumour (McDonnell, Mc Donald, Behan & Gibney, J Endocr Soc 2021; PMC8266101). The paraneoplastic cascade is logical: hCG mimics LH and stimulates Leydig cells (high T); that T is aromatised to massive estradiol (gynecomastia, gonadotropin suppression); and at high titres hCG cross-reacts with the TSH receptor causing thyrotoxicosis without autoimmunity. Two traps: the urine hCG was falsely negative from the high-dose 'hook effect', so always order quantitative serum β-hCG when titres may be astronomical; and the right treatment is upfront BEP chemotherapy with a β-blocker for symptoms — not thionamides, because hCG hyperthyroidism resolves as tumour burden falls (β-hCG fell from 504,000 to 65 over 2 months and all endocrinopathies resolved). A young man with several simultaneous endocrinopathies is a trophoblastic/germ-cell tumour until proven otherwise.
keyPoints: hCG → Leydig stimulation (high T) → aromatisation (high E2, gynecomastia) + TSH-receptor cross-reactivity (thyrotoxicosis)
High-dose hook effect makes urine hCG falsely negative — order quantitative SERUM β-hCG
Treat with chemotherapy (BEP) + β-blocker; thionamides unnecessary — hCG hyperthyroidism resolves with tumour control

items.title: How prepubertal testes with virilization localize an extragonadal hCG source (Case 19)
items.content: A 2.5-year-old boy had penile enlargement, pubic hair, acne, voice change and a hepatic mass, with adult-range testosterone (380 ng/dL), suppressed LH/FSH, a flat LH response to GnRH agonist (confirming gonadotropin-independent precocious puberty), β-hCG 24,800 IU/L and AFP 480,000 ng/mL — an hCG-secreting hepatoblastoma (Eren et al., J Clin Res Pediatr Endocrinol 2010; PMC3005758). The cardinal localising sign is the testes: in peripheral precocious puberty driven by an extragonadal hCG source, the boy virilises but the testes stay prepubertal (3 mL) because the hCG drive is systemic, not a primary gonadal LH signal that would enlarge them. So peripheral precocious puberty in a boy means look beyond the testes — hCG-secreting hepatoblastoma, dysgerminoma, choriocarcinoma, mediastinal teratoma, pineal germinoma. AFP >100,000 with a hepatic mass in a child under 3 is hepatoblastoma until proven otherwise; treatment is neoadjuvant cisplatin/doxorubicin then resection, with markers normalising by 6 months.
keyPoints: Virilization with PREPUBERTAL testes = extragonadal hCG source, not central precocious puberty
Flat LH on GnRH-agonist test confirms gonadotropin-independent (peripheral) precocious puberty
AFP >100,000 + hepatic mass in a child <3 = hepatoblastoma until proven otherwise

items.title: Why an older male smoker with gynecomastia and high β-hCG needs a chest scan (Case 20)
items.content: A 65-year-old man (50 pack-years) had painful gynecomastia, lost libido and a dry cough, with elevated β-hCG, high estradiol (~460 pmol/L) and high testosterone, normal scrotal ultrasound, and bilateral lung masses plus a solitary brain metastasis — a β-hCG-secreting pulmonary choriocarcinoma (dedifferentiated NSCLC variant, syncytiotrophoblast-like β-hCG-positive giant cells) (Devos et al., Case Rep Oncol Med 2024; PMC10838210). Ectopic β-hCG is documented in up to half of NSCLCs but rarely produces symptomatic gynecomastia. When β-hCG is raised in an older male smoker without a testicular mass, the differential is pulmonary, gastric, renal-pelvis, hepatic, pancreatic and bladder carcinomas, extragonadal (mediastinal/retroperitoneal) germ-cell tumours, and the very rare pituitary β-hCG-producing adenoma. The work-up order matters: image the testes first, then the chest. Falling β-hCG tracks treatment response — here whole-brain radiotherapy plus carboplatin/etoposide/pembrolizumab improved both the cancer and the gynecomastia.
keyPoints: Older male smoker + gynecomastia + high β-hCG, normal testes → image the chest (and abdomen)
Ectopic β-hCG occurs in up to 50% of NSCLC but rarely causes symptomatic gynecomastia
Image testes first, then chest; β-hCG is a useful response marker

items.title: Why therapeutic hCG causes gynecomastia and how to manage it (Bonus case)
items.content: A 28-year-old man with Kallmann syndrome on hCG 1500 IU twice weekly plus recombinant FSH for fertility developed tender bilateral gynecomastia at month 4, with estradiol 78 pg/mL (representative vignette drawn from the gonadotropin-therapy literature). Gynecomastia is the commonest side-effect of hCG therapy (~10–20%): supraphysiologic Leydig-cell stimulation raises intratesticular testosterone and drives both intratesticular and peripheral aromatisation, lifting the E2:T ratio. The graded management is to lower the hCG dose first, add a low-dose aromatase inhibitor (anastrozole 0.5 mg twice weekly) to bring down the E2:T ratio, and a SERM (tamoxifen) if breast symptoms persist — here gynecomastia regressed 60% by month 9 and his partner conceived at month 11. The same physiology applies to non-medical hCG use in TRT clinics and the discredited Simeons hCG weight-loss diet, so counsel before starting.
keyPoints: hCG therapy → ↑ intratesticular T → ↑ aromatisation → gynecomastia in ~10–20%
Manage by lowering hCG dose, adding a low-dose aromatase inhibitor, then a SERM if symptomatic
Counsel before starting; same risk with non-medical/weight-loss hCG use

items.title: How to work through suspected estrogen excess — the master algorithm
items.content: Work in four steps. Step 1 — confirm true glandular tissue: pinch test and supine palpation, and exclude pseudogynecomastia and male breast cancer (mammography if asymmetric/hard/fixed). Step 2 — targeted history: drugs (AAS, finasteride, spironolactone, digoxin, ketoconazole, PPIs, antiretrovirals), environmental exposures (transdermal HRT in the household, cosmetics, food), family history (AEXS, PJS), and weight/alcohol/refeeding. Step 3 — first-tier labs: LH, FSH, total and free testosterone, estradiol (LC-MS/MS if available), SHBG, prolactin, TSH/free T4, β-hCG, AFP, LFTs and renal function. Then branch on the pattern: β-hCG high → scrotal ultrasound, and if normal, CT chest/abdomen (germ-cell, hepatic, lung); estradiol high with suppressed gonadotropins and normal hCG → scrotal ultrasound (Leydig/Sertoli), adrenal CT/MRI (feminizing ACC) and an LC-MS/MS steroid profile; estradiol normal/high with low T but HIGH gonadotropins → primary hypogonadism (Klinefelter, mumps orchitis, aging); a pre-/peri-pubertal child with a dominant family history → chromosomal microarray for 15q21 (AEXS); a drug/obesity/refeeding cause identified → remove it and observe. Step 4 — definitive treatment: remove the agent/tumour; SERM for gynecomastia under 12 months; aromatase inhibitor for AEXS, LCCSCT and TRT/hCG-induced disease; surgery for fibrotic gynecomastia over 12 months.
keyPoints: Confirm glandular tissue (exclude pseudogynecomastia/cancer) → targeted drug/environmental/family history → first-tier labs
Branch on the pattern: high β-hCG vs high-E2/suppressed-gonadotropins vs high-gonadotropin primary hypogonadism vs AEXS
Treat by cause: remove agent/tumour; SERM <12 months; AI for AEXS/LCCSCT/iatrogenic; surgery for fibrosis

items.title: Treatment rules at a glance — remove the cause, SERM, aromatase inhibitor, or surgery
items.content: Map each bucket to its definitive action. Exogenous estrogen / environmental: remove the source (occlusive patch, hygiene, dietary change) — regression in months. Aromatizable androgens / AAS: stop the steroid, restore fertility with hCG+hMG if needed, SERM early before fibrosis. Drug-induced (spironolactone/digoxin/finasteride): substitute the drug (eplerenone for spironolactone), SERM if <12 months, surgery if fibrotic. Peripheral aromatase (obesity/aging/refeeding): weight loss/time; tamoxifen for symptoms; surgery only after the driver is corrected. AEXS: aromatase inhibitor (letrozole/anastrozole) ± GH to preserve height. Estrogen-secreting tumours: surgical excision (adrenalectomy, testis-sparing or radical orchiectomy, TAH-BSO) ± adjuvant therapy by pathology; AI for LCCSCT. hCG-secreting tumours: chemotherapy (BEP/PLADO) and let hCG-driven endocrinopathies resolve with tumour control. Therapeutic hCG: lower dose, add low-dose AI, SERM if symptomatic. Timing rule throughout: SERM (tamoxifen 10–20 mg/day or raloxifene) works for painful, non-fibrotic gynecomastia under ~12 months; established fibrotic gynecomastia over 12 months needs surgery.
keyPoints: Cause-specific: remove exposure / substitute drug / weight loss / AI / excise tumour / chemo for hCG tumours
Restore AAS-related fertility with hCG+hMG; eplerenone replaces spironolactone
SERM <12 months and non-fibrotic; surgery for fibrotic disease >12 months

items.title: Module appraisal — strengths, limitations, and assumptions
items.content: Strength: organising estrogen excess by the seven-bucket framework and anchoring each bucket to a published case gives a reproducible pattern-recognition tool that scales from the trivially reversible (a parent's gel, a drug, obesity) to the lethal (feminizing ACC, hCG-secreting germ-cell tumour), all read off the same axes — E2:T ratio, gonadotropins, and β-hCG/AFP. Limitations to flag as doubt: most sources are individual case reports, one is a conference abstract (Hummel, ESPE 2022) and several scenarios are 'representative' syntheses (e.g., the aging and refeeding vignettes from Endotext), so they teach mechanism and approach, not incidence or effect size. Reported hormone values span different assays and eras and are illustrative, not thresholds — and a recurring theme of the module is exactly that immunoassay estradiol misleads, so confirm unexpected values (especially in boys and when ACC is suspected) by LC-MS/MS. Assumptions: that true glandular tissue and male breast cancer have been addressed first, and that the dangerous tumoural causes are actively excluded rather than presumed. Drug names, doses and regimens here are educational, not prescriptive, and several treatments (AI in children, testis-sparing surgery, deferring mitotane) are individualised, evidence-evolving decisions for specialist multidisciplinary care. Citation note: every cited case was checked against PubMed/PMC and several author attributions in the source compilation were corrected to the verified authors; the therapeutic-hCG 'bonus' case is a representative vignette drawn from the gonadotropin-therapy literature rather than a single discrete case report.
keyPoints: Strong for pattern recognition and approach; case reports/abstract — not incidence or effect size
Hormone numbers are illustrative across assays/eras; confirm unexpected estradiol by LC-MS/MS
Assumes glandular tissue/breast cancer addressed and tumours actively excluded; treatments are educational, specialist-individualised

items.question: Why does a bodybuilder on high-dose testosterone become infertile (azoospermic) despite a sky-high serum testosterone?
items.answer: Because spermatogenesis depends on intratesticular testosterone and FSH, not on circulating serum testosterone. Exogenous androgen feeds back to suppress hypothalamic GnRH and pituitary LH and FSH; without LH the Leydig cells stop producing the very high local (intratesticular) testosterone concentrations that the seminiferous tubules need, and without FSH the Sertoli-cell support of spermatogenesis fails. So even though serum testosterone is enormous from the injected drug, the intratesticular level collapses and sperm production stops — hence azoospermia with testicular atrophy. Recovery exploits the same physiology in reverse: hCG mimics LH to drive Leydig cells and restore intratesticular testosterone, while hMG (or recombinant FSH) supplies the FSH signal for the Sertoli cells. In the published case this resumed spermatogenesis at 4 months and achieved conception by 9 months, but counsel that recovery is slow (6–24 months) and not guaranteed.
keyPoints: Sperm production needs intratesticular T + FSH, not serum T
Exogenous androgen suppresses LH/FSH → intratesticular T collapses despite high serum T → azoospermia
Restore with hCG (LH-like, drives intratesticular T) + hMG/FSH; slow, not guaranteed

items.question: Why can a patient have clear gynecomastia while the serum estradiol reads normal?
items.answer: Because gynecomastia is driven by the estrogen-to-androgen balance at the breast tissue itself, which a single serum estradiol does not capture. Three situations produce normal serum E2 with real breast stimulation. First, a ratio shift: if testosterone falls (drugs, hypogonadism, SHBG changes) the E2:T ratio rises even with an unchanged absolute estradiol. Second, local (paracrine) aromatase: tumours such as large-cell calcifying Sertoli-cell tumours, or 'spared' testosterone aromatised in breast tissue after 5α-reductase inhibition (finasteride), generate estrogen action where it matters without raising circulating levels much. Third, assay error: conventional estradiol immunoassays are unreliable at low male/childhood concentrations and cross-react with other steroids, so a 'normal' value may be falsely reassuring — LC-MS/MS is the better test. The practical lesson is to interpret estradiol alongside testosterone, gonadotropins and the clinical picture, not as a standalone number.
keyPoints: Gynecomastia tracks the tissue E2:T ratio, not absolute serum E2
Local/paracrine aromatase (LCCSCT, post-finasteride) acts without raising circulating E2
Immunoassays mislead at low levels — use LC-MS/MS and read E2 with T, gonadotropins, context

items.question: How does the gonadotropin (LH/FSH) pattern help localise the cause of estrogen excess?
items.answer: The gonadotropins tell you whether the gonad is being over-driven, switched off, or failing. Suppressed LH and FSH point to an autonomous source feeding back on the axis: exogenous estrogen or aromatizable androgen, an estrogen-secreting tumour (Leydig, Sertoli, feminizing ACC), or aromatase excess — here estradiol is inappropriately high or normal for the low testosterone. High LH and FSH with low testosterone indicate primary hypogonadism (Klinefelter, mumps orchitis, aging), where relative estrogen excess comes from the altered ratio. A high β-hCG that behaves like LH (stimulating Leydig cells, often with very high testosterone) reframes the picture toward a trophoblastic/germ-cell, hepatic or pulmonary tumour — and remember the hook effect can make urine hCG falsely negative, so order quantitative serum β-hCG. Combined with estradiol, testosterone, β-hCG/AFP and imaging, the gonadotropin pattern is the single most useful branch point in the algorithm.
keyPoints: Suppressed LH/FSH = autonomous estrogen/androgen source (exogenous, tumour, AEXS)
High LH/FSH + low T = primary hypogonadism (ratio-driven relative estrogen excess)
hCG acting LH-like (often very high T) = trophoblastic/germ-cell/hepatic/lung tumour; use serum β-hCG

items.question: How do you distinguish peripheral (gonadotropin-independent) from central precocious puberty in a virilized boy?
items.answer: Use the testicular volume and the gonadotropin axis. In central (true) precocious puberty the pituitary drives the gonad, so LH/FSH are pubertal and the testes enlarge symmetrically (>4 mL) in step with virilization, and a GnRH-agonist stimulation test gives a brisk (pubertal) LH response. In peripheral precocious puberty the androgen/estrogen comes from elsewhere — an adrenal or gonadal tumour, exogenous hormone, AEXS, or an hCG-secreting tumour — so the boy virilises while LH/FSH are suppressed and the GnRH-agonist test shows a flat LH response. The cardinal bedside clue is the mismatch: marked virilization with prepubertal-sized testes means the drive is not pituitary LH. An asymmetrically enlarged single testis suggests a Leydig/Sertoli tumour; bilaterally prepubertal testes with high β-hCG point to an extragonadal hCG source such as hepatoblastoma. Bone age and the β-hCG/AFP/estradiol panel then complete the localisation.
keyPoints: Central PP: pubertal LH/FSH, testes >4 mL, brisk GnRH-agonist LH response
Peripheral PP: suppressed LH/FSH, prepubertal testes despite virilization, flat GnRH-agonist response
Virilization + prepubertal testes = extragonadal/peripheral source; check β-hCG/AFP and image

items.stem: A 24-year-old man has bilateral painful gynecomastia, β-hCG >500,000 IU/L, free T4 30 pmol/L, suppressed TSH, and a left testicular mass. The most appropriate immediate management is:
items.explanation: This is hCG-mediated thyrotoxicosis from a metastatic germ-cell tumour. The hyperthyroidism is driven by hCG cross-reacting with the TSH receptor and resolves as tumour burden falls, so a β-blocker controls symptoms while upfront BEP chemotherapy treats the cancer. Thionamides are unnecessary and delay oncological treatment, radioiodine and thyroidectomy are inappropriate, and orchiectomy is done after chemotherapy. (Case 18)

items.stem: A 35-year-old man developed gynecomastia 6 months after starting finasteride 1 mg/day for hair loss; he stopped the drug 5 months ago with no regression. The best next step is:
items.explanation: A SERM (tamoxifen) is first-line for early drug-induced gynecomastia that is painful and not yet densely fibrotic. Re-challenge would re-expose him to the cause, an aromatase inhibitor alone is not the established first-line for this scenario, and surgery is reserved for SERM failure or established fibrosis beyond ~12 months. (Case 7)

items.stem: A 7-year-old boy with Peutz–Jeghers syndrome develops bilateral B3 gynecomastia; testicular ultrasound shows bilateral microcalcifications. The most likely diagnosis is:
items.explanation: Large-cell calcifying Sertoli-cell tumour (LCCSCT) is the classic Peutz–Jeghers-associated gonadal tumour and is virtually pathognomonic when bilateral testicular microcalcifications accompany prepubertal gynecomastia; it overexpresses aromatase locally, so estradiol may be only minimally elevated. The others do not fit the PJS-plus-microcalcification picture. (Case 16)

items.stem: A 24-year-old man has gynecomastia, estradiol 90 pg/mL, suppressed gonadotropins, an 8-cm right adrenal mass, and inappropriately preserved cortisol despite suppressed ACTH. The best initial treatment is:
items.explanation: Complete (R0) surgical resection is the cornerstone of feminizing adrenocortical carcinoma management. Mitotane (± EDP chemotherapy) is reserved for high-risk pathology (e.g., Ki-67 >20%, vascular invasion) or unresectable/recurrent disease, an aromatase inhibitor does not treat the tumour, and observation is unsafe for a likely malignant mass. (Case 13)

items.stem: Which hormonal/clinical pattern is most consistent with aromatase excess syndrome (AEXS)?
options: High LH, high FSH, high testosterone, high estradiol
Low LH, low FSH, low testosterone, normal/high estradiol with an autosomal dominant family history
Low LH, low FSH, high testosterone, high estradiol in a bodybuilder
Elevated hCG with bilateral testicular masses
items.explanation: AEXS is peripheral hyperaromatisation from a CYP19A1 rearrangement: the autonomously generated estrogen suppresses gonadotropins, so LH/FSH and testosterone are low while estradiol is inappropriately normal or high, classically with peripubertal gynecomastia, advanced bone age and a dominant family history. The bodybuilder pattern reflects exogenous androgen; high gonadotropins indicate primary hypogonadism; hCG with testicular masses indicates a germ-cell tumour. (Cases 11–12)

items.stem: A urine hCG is negative in a man with a 13-cm retroperitoneal mass and gynecomastia, but suspicion of a trophoblastic tumour is high. What is the most likely explanation?
options: The result correctly excludes an hCG-secreting tumour
The high-dose hook effect at very high β-hCG titres
items.explanation: At astronomical β-hCG concentrations the qualitative urine assay can be falsely negative because antibody-binding sites are saturated (the high-dose 'hook' effect). The fix is to order a quantitative serum β-hCG (with dilution). Imaging is not the issue, and heterophile interference is not the classic explanation for this pattern. (Case 18)

items.stem: The typical hormonal pattern of an obese 14-year-old boy with gynecomastia is:
options: High testosterone, high estradiol, high LH
Low SHBG, low free testosterone, elevated estradiol, increased aromatase activity
High SHBG, high free testosterone, low estradiol
items.explanation: Obesity increases adipose aromatase mass and activity and lowers SHBG, so estradiol rises, free testosterone falls, and the free E2:T ratio climbs at the breast. The other options describe patterns that are physiologically inconsistent with obesity-driven gynecomastia or point to a tumour. (Case 8)

items.stem: A 56-year-old postmenopausal woman has heavy vaginal bleeding, estradiol 168 pg/mL, FSH 3 IU/L, and atypical endometrial hyperplasia. The most useful tumour marker(s) for the suspected ovarian tumour is/are:
items.explanation: The picture — postmenopausal bleeding with high estradiol and an inappropriately suppressed FSH — suggests an estrogen-secreting adult granulosa-cell tumour, for which inhibin B and AMH are the most sensitive markers and outperform CA-125 for detection and recurrence. CEA and AFP are not granulosa-cell markers. (Case 17)

items.stem: A previously healthy 20-year-old man on intramuscular testosterone cypionate 500 mg/week for 5 years presents with gynecomastia. The expected hormonal profile is:
options: High testosterone, high LH, high FSH, low estradiol
High testosterone, suppressed LH, suppressed FSH, high estradiol
Low testosterone, high LH, low estradiol
Normal testosterone, normal estradiol, normal LH
items.explanation: Exogenous testosterone raises serum testosterone, is aromatised to estradiol (driving gynecomastia), and suppresses the HPG axis so LH and FSH are low. High testosterone with suppressed gonadotropins is the fingerprint of an exogenous androgen source. (Cases 4–5)

items.stem: A young prepubertal boy who sits daily on the lap of a transgender parent using estradiol gel develops gynecomastia and peripheral precocious puberty. The first step in management is:
options: Eliminate the exposure — change formulation (gel→patch), hygiene, and contact precautions
items.explanation: Removing the source is first-line and usually sufficient: switch the parent from gel to an occlusive patch, enforce hand-washing and covered application sites, and avoid skin contact for ≥4 hours after dosing. Symptoms regress over 3–6 months, so aromatase inhibitors, SERMs and surgery are not needed. (Case 2)

items.statement: Transdermal estradiol gel transfers to a child through skin contact, whereas estradiol patches and oral routes do not transfer.
items.explanation: True. The gel formulation leaves active hormone on the skin surface that transfers during contact, which is why secondary exposure in children is a recognised hazard; occlusive patches and oral routes do not transfer. Switching a gel to a patch is a standard mitigation. (Cases 1–2)

items.statement: Finasteride-induced gynecomastia always resolves once the drug is stopped.
items.explanation: False. Pharmacovigilance data show roughly 20% of finasteride-associated gynecomastia does not regress after stopping; once the tissue becomes fibrotic (typically beyond ~12 months) it is effectively irreversible and needs surgery. Early SERM treatment is the way to avoid this. (Case 7)

items.statement: Eplerenone is a rational substitute for spironolactone in a man who develops gynecomastia, because eplerenone does not bind the androgen receptor.
items.explanation: True. Spironolactone causes gynecomastia partly by blocking the androgen receptor (and by lowering testosterone and displacing it from SHBG). Eplerenone is a selective mineralocorticoid-receptor antagonist that does not bind the androgen receptor, so it provides aldosterone blockade with far less gynecomastia. (Case 6)

items.statement: In a boy with virilization from an hCG-secreting hepatoblastoma, the testes are typically enlarged (>4 mL) because hCG stimulates the gonad.
items.explanation: False. The hallmark of an extragonadal hCG source is virilization with PREPUBERTAL-sized testes, because the drive is systemic hCG rather than a primary pituitary/gonadal LH signal that would enlarge the testes. Prepubertal testes with virilization should send you looking beyond the gonad. (Case 19)

items.assertion: In hCG-mediated thyrotoxicosis from a germ-cell tumour, antithyroid drugs (thionamides) are the definitive treatment.
items.reason: At high titres hCG cross-reacts with the TSH receptor to stimulate thyroid hormone synthesis.
items.explanation: The reason is true — high-titre hCG agonises the TSH receptor and causes thyrotoxicosis without autoimmunity. But the assertion is false: the definitive treatment is tumour control (chemotherapy), with a β-blocker for symptoms, because the hyperthyroidism resolves as β-hCG falls; thionamides are not definitive. A false assertion with a true reason is option 3. (Case 18)

items.assertion: In aromatase excess syndrome, LH and FSH are suppressed.
items.reason: Peripherally generated estrogen exerts negative feedback on the hypothalamic–pituitary axis.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. In AEXS, constitutive peripheral aromatisation raises estrogen, which feeds back to suppress gonadotropin secretion — so LH, FSH and testosterone are low while estradiol is inappropriately normal/high. Hence option 0. (Cases 11–12)

items.assertion: Finasteride-induced gynecomastia always resolves completely once the drug is discontinued.
items.reason: Inhibition of 5α-reductase by finasteride is reversible, with enzyme activity recovering after the drug is stopped.
items.explanation: The reason is true — the enzyme inhibition itself is reversible. But the assertion is false: a substantial minority of cases do not regress because established glandular tissue fibroses and becomes irreversible, regardless of enzyme recovery. A false assertion with a true reason is option 3. (Case 7)

items.question: List the seven etiologic 'buckets' of estrogen excess with one example of each.
items.answer: (1) Exogenous estrogens / ER agonists — transdermal HRT transfer, estrogen-contaminated food. (2) Aromatizable androgens and digitalis — anabolic-androgenic steroid abuse; digoxin (weak ER agonist). (3) Increased peripheral aromatase activity — obesity, aging, refeeding, cirrhosis. (4) Familial/genetic aromatase excess (AEXS) — CYP19A1 15q21 rearrangements. (5) Estrogen-secreting tumours — feminizing adrenocortical carcinoma, Leydig-cell, Sertoli-cell (LCCSCT), ovarian granulosa-cell tumour. (6) hCG-secreting tumours — germ-cell tumour, hepatoblastoma, lung/urothelial carcinoma. (7) Therapeutic hCG — fertility induction or hypogonadism treatment.

items.question: Name the common drugs to ask about when a man presents with gynecomastia.
items.answer: Anabolic-androgenic steroids and exogenous testosterone; finasteride/dutasteride (5α-reductase inhibitors); spironolactone; digoxin/digitalis; cimetidine and other H2-blockers; proton-pump inhibitors; calcium-channel blockers; ketoconazole; antiretrovirals; and exposure to transdermal estrogen products (HRT/gender-affirming gels) in the household. Also ask about alcohol, marijuana, and herbal/cosmetic phytoestrogens (lavender/tea-tree oil, soy). Therapeutic or non-medical hCG (TRT clinics, weight-loss protocols) belongs on the list too.

items.question: When is medical (SERM) therapy appropriate for gynecomastia, and when is surgery required?
items.answer: A SERM (tamoxifen 10–20 mg/day, or raloxifene 60 mg/day) is first-line for early gynecomastia — painful, recent-onset (under about 12 months) and not yet densely fibrotic — with response rates of roughly 60–80%, ideally after removing any offending drug or cause. Once gynecomastia is long-standing (over ~12 months) the glandular tissue fibroses and no longer responds to medical therapy, so surgery (subcutaneous mastectomy ± liposuction, or reduction mammoplasty) becomes the definitive option. For physiologic causes (obesity, refeeding) correct the driver first and operate only on the residual core after weight/state stabilises.
